# GitHub for Beginners

Chapter 9 connected your repository to a remote and showed that the Git
half of GitHub is just `push`, `pull`, `clone`. This chapter is about
the other half: the website. GitHub wraps a hosted Git repository in a
project home page, a README that renders on arrival, a license the
platform can detect, an issue tracker, and a file browser with full
history. For a researcher this layer is what turns a private backup into
something a coauthor, a referee, or a stranger replicating your paper
can actually use.

Unlike the rest of the book, this chapter cannot show captured terminal
output for its main subject, because the subject is a web interface.
GitHub's UI also changes more often than Git's commands do. So the web
steps here are described from GitHub's current documentation (verified
2026-06-22, logged in `verification/2026-06-22-ch9-14-github-sources.md`)
and flagged as documented-not-captured; button labels may have moved by
the time you read this. The book's own repository,
`github.com/tensor-polinomics/git-github-guide`, is the live worked
example throughout. Where a command can stand in for a click, a
read-only `gh` box gives you one.

## Creating a repository {.tier-beginner}

There are two ways onto GitHub, and beginners regularly trip on the
difference.

The **empty-then-push** path matches Chapter 9: create a *bare* repo on
GitHub (do not let it add anything), then `git remote add origin` and
`git push -u origin main` from your existing local project. This is the
right path when the project already exists on your laptop, which for a
research project it usually does.

The **initialize-on-GitHub** path is for starting fresh. On the New
Repository page you give the repo a name, choose Public or Private, and
optionally tick three starter files: a **README**, a **.gitignore**
chosen from a template list, and a **license** chosen from a picker.
GitHub then makes a first commit containing those files, and you
`git clone` the result down to start working.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Do not mix the two paths. If you initialize the GitHub repo with a
README *and* you already have local commits, the two histories have no
common ancestor, and your first `git push` is rejected as
non-fast-forward (the same rejection Chapter 12 covers). The fix is to
`git pull --no-rebase origin main --allow-unrelated-histories`, resolve
any conflict, then push, but the cleaner habit is: existing project ->
create the GitHub repo empty; brand-new project -> initialize on GitHub
and clone. Pick one per project.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**The same thing without the web UI (`gh`).** The GitHub CLI creates a
repository from the terminal, with the same starter-file options as the
web form. This is a state-changing command, so it sits here on its own,
to run deliberately, not in a copy-paste block:

```bash
gh repo create minwage-did --public --source=. \
  --remote=origin --push
```

`--source=.` pushes the current local project (the empty-then-push
path); add `--add-readme`, `--gitignore Python`, or `--license mit` to
take the initialize-on-GitHub path instead.
:::

## The README is the front page {.tier-beginner}

GitHub renders the file named `README.md` directly on the repository's
home page, so it is the first and often only thing a visitor reads. For
research code it is not decoration; it is the entry point to
reproducing your work. A README that earns its place answers, briefly:
what this project is (one line, the paper it backs), how to set up the
environment (the `uv`/`renv` lockfile from Chapter 8), how to reproduce
the results (the exact command sequence: make data, clean, analyse), and
where the data come from if they are not in the repo. The running
example's README does exactly this, which is why a cloner can rebuild
the headline estimate without ever talking to you.

## A license is not optional for reuse {.tier-beginner}

Here is the part most researchers get wrong: code with no license is
**not** free to use. Under copyright law the default is all-rights-
reserved, so a repository with no `LICENSE` file is one that, strictly,
nobody else may legally copy, modify, or build on, however public it
looks. If you want people to replicate and extend your work, which is
the entire point of posting it, you must say so with a license.

GitHub's picker (offered when you create the repo) lists the common
choices; `choosealicense.com` explains them in plain language. For
research, the usual split is a permissive software license for the code
and a Creative Commons license for the prose and figures. This book's
own repository does exactly that: **MIT** for the code, **CC BY 4.0**
for the writing and diagrams, both files at the repository root.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Reproducibility has a legal dimension that is easy to miss. A journal's
data editor, a replicator, or a future student can only *use* your code
if its license permits it. "It is on GitHub" is not permission. Add a
`LICENSE` file at the start, not as an afterthought: pick a permissive
license (MIT or BSD-3-Clause are standard for research code) so reuse is
unambiguous, and license data and text separately if they have
different terms. The license is part of the replication package
(Chapter 15), not a formality.
:::

## Issues: the project's lab notebook {.tier-beginner}

An **issue** is a titled, threaded note attached to the repository:
a bug, a task, a question, a decision to revisit. For solo research it
works as a running lab notebook and todo list ("placebo test for the
2019 cohort", "referee 2 wants clustered SEs"); for a team it is where
work is proposed, discussed, and assigned before anyone writes code.
Each issue gets a number, and that number is the glue: writing
`Fixes #14` in a commit message or pull request links the two, and
merging it can close the issue automatically. Months later, the issue
thread is the *why* behind a change that the diff alone cannot tell you.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Read-only issue inspection (`gh`).** Listing and reading issues
changes nothing, so these are safe to run directly. Creating one
(`gh issue create`) is state-changing and is left for you to do
deliberately.

```bash
gh issue list                 # open issues in this repo
gh issue view 14              # one issue and its thread
```
:::

## Reading a repository on the web {.tier-advanced}

The web UI is genuinely useful for *reading* history, and worth knowing
beyond the home page. The file browser shows the tree at any commit. Any
file has a **History** view (the commits that touched it) and a **Blame**
view (which commit last changed each line, the web version of
`git blame` from Chapter 7's debugging toolkit). Each commit has its own
page showing its full diff. Pressing `y` on a file rewrites the URL to a
**permalink** pinned to the current commit hash, which is the link to
paste in a paper or an email, because a plain branch URL silently
changes meaning as `main` moves. None of this is editing; it is the
fastest way to answer "when did this number change, and who changed it"
without leaving the browser.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
GitHub lets you edit files and commit straight from the web (the pencil
icon), which is convenient and a trap for analysis code. A web edit
commits directly to the branch without your local environment, so
nothing was re-run and nothing was tested: it is the perfect way to
commit a typo that breaks the pipeline. Reserve web edits for prose
(README, docs); make code changes locally where you can run them first.
:::

## What you can now do

You can create a repository the right way for your situation
(empty-then-push for existing work, initialize-on-GitHub for a fresh
start), write a README that lets a stranger reproduce your results,
attach a license so they are legally allowed to, track tasks and
decisions as issues that link to the commits that resolve them, and read
a project's history on the web with History, Blame, and permalinks. That
is enough to run a solo project entirely in the open. Chapter 11 adds
the collaboration machinery: pull requests, review, forks, protected
branches, and automation.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Using the running example and a free GitHub account:

1. Create a new repository on GitHub, initialized with a README, a
   Python `.gitignore`, and the MIT license. Clone it, and confirm the
   three files are present and that GitHub shows "MIT License" on the
   home page.
2. Replace the generated README with one that states what the project
   is and the exact commands to reproduce the headline estimate. Commit
   and push, then reload the home page to see it render.
3. Open an issue titled "Add clustered standard errors". In a commit on
   a branch, write `Refs #1` in the message, push, and confirm GitHub
   links the commit to the issue.
4. Open any file's Blame view and find the commit that last changed the
   headline-result line. Press `y` to get its permalink and note how the
   URL changes.
5. Explain in one sentence why a public repository with no `LICENSE`
   file is still not free for others to reuse.
